question: Imagine your school invites Malala to preside over ‘Children’s Day’ celebration programme. Draft a welcome speech for this guest of honour.
answer:
Celebrating Children’s Day
Good morning, and a warm welcome to everyone present today. On the occasion of Children’s Day, I, Ms Kritika Sharma, would like to welcome Ms Malala Yousafzai to preside as the Chief Guest for today. s celebrations.
Ms Malala is a human rights advocate who has worked constantly to provide education opportunities and better infrastructure to girls from underprivileged and war-struck nations. Her entry into the field of social work is preceded by a tragic incident. A few years ago, Malala was just another 15-year-old student on a school bus with her friends. On 9th Oct 2012, armed Taliban men boarded that school bus and shot Malaia in the head. This tragic incident changed her life forever. She endured traumatic operations in Pakistan. In her famous speech at the UN, nine months later, she said, “They thought that the bullets would silence us, but they failed. And then, out of that silence, came thousands of voices... weakness, fear and hopelessness died. Strength, power and courage were born”.
Malala’s story is that of strength, courage and determination. So, please put your hands together for this angel of education. We are privileged to have her celebrate this occasion with us.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March

question: Get into pairs/groups and match the columns: ‘A’ (1) Science (2) Religion (3) Spirituality (4) Philosophy (5) Theology
answer:
‘A’
‘B’

(1) Science
The intellectual and practical activities in a systematic study of structure and behaviour of the natural world through experiment and observation.

(2) Religion
The belief in and worship of a superhuman controlling power God.

(3) Spirituality
The quality of being more concerned with the human spirit as opposed to material or physical things.

(4) Philosophy
The study of the basic nature of knowledge, reality and existence, especially as an academic subject.

(5) Theology
The study of nature of God and religious belief.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: What’s the difference between science and technology? Discuss in pairs and tell your answer to the class.
answer: We generally assume that science and technology mean almost the same thing. However, there is a difference. The goal of science is the pursuit of knowledge for its own sake, while the goal of technology is to create products that solve problems and improve human life. Simply put, technology is the practical application of science.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Do you think that science and faith are both important in our lives? Why? Discuss in your group and give a small presentation in front of the class.
answer: Yes, both science and faith are important in our lives. There is no contradiction between the two. Both genuinely seek the truth. Science explores the truth about the body and the physical world. Faith reflects on the nature of the soul. Science helps us understand the physical structure of the universe, while faith deals with human values and morals. Both complement each other. In the words of Einstein. Science without religion is lame; religion without science is blind.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: We need science to understand latest technology We need faith to enable our spirit to soar high.
answer:
We need science

We need faith
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Refer to a dictionary to find out the meanings of the following words. Spirit - a person’s mind or feelings or soul. Spiritual Spirituality Spiritualism Spiritualist
answer:
Spiritual: relating to or affecting the human spirit or soul, as opposed to material or physical things
Spirituality: the quality of being concerned with the human spirit, or soul, as opposed to material or physical things.
Spiritualism: a system of belief or religious practice based on supposed communication with the spirits of the dead, especially through mediums
Spiritualist: an advocate of the doctrine that the spirit exists as distinct from matter, or that spirit is the only reality
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: How did the Reverend relate and compare the work that he and Dr. Sarabhai did for people?
answer: The Reverend said that Dr. Sarabhai was a scientist. We benefit from the devices that science has developed in many ways, in our homes and in the fields of medicine and technology. A priest prays for the peace and well-being of his people. Thus, both science and spirituality seek the Almighty’s blessing for the prosperity of the human mind and body. In short, both are doing the same job.

question: Why is there an urgent need to replace ‘What can I take’ with ‘What can I give’?
answer: There is an urgent need to replace ‘What can I take’ with ‘What can I give’ for the very survival of humanity. Unless we realize the gravity of the message, this planet will become a hub of environmental degradation, social evils, inequity, and corruption. If we do not take up the challenge, our planet will become uninhabitable, and the human race will not prosper.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: In your notebook, write a short paragraph on the following covering up their profession and personality traits with examples. Rev. Peter Bernard Pereira
answer: Rev. Father Bernard Pereira was the priest-in-charge of the church in Thumba, Kerala. He was a person who immediately understood the dilemma in the minds of Dr. Kalam and Professor Sarabhai. He realised that he would have to make a sacrifice in the interests of science. At the same time, he could not do this without his congregation’s approval. So he put the concept before the entire gathering in forceful terms and secured unanimous support for establishing the space research centre.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: In your notebook, write a short paragraph on the following covering up their profession and personality traits with examples. Dr. Sri Sri Shivakumar Mahaswamiji
answer: Dr Sri Sri Shivakumara Mahaswamiji was the Head of an ashram in Turnkur, Karnataka. He was a true humanitarian who believed in doing good for people. With that in mind, he established a free residential education system for over 9000 children in the ashram. His strength came from being a ‘giver’, and even at the ripe age of 102, he stood steady and alert without any support. He would give a wise discourse for half an hour, then go on to feed thousands of children. He was indeed a remarkable personality.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: In your notebook, write a short paragraph on the following covering up their profession and personality traits with examples. Dr. A. P. J. Abdul Kalam
answer: Dr. A. P. J. Abdul Kalam was a great scientist. He was so humble and he said that he got this humility from his father. Dr. Kalam joined ISRO in 1960 when it was just a fledgling organisation. His interactions with the great scientist Professor Vikram Sarabhai and the Reverend Peter Bernard Pereira shaped his thoughts on religion and taught him about the true meaning of religious service. From Professor Dhavan, he learned how one can be academically accomplished. From Mahaswamiji, he realised the value of generosity. He learned many good qualities from great people like Pramukh Swami, Mahapragya Ji, Professor Brahma Prakash, Father Pereira, Dr. Sarabhai, Swami Sivananda of Rishikesh, and his father. He believed that faith and fact can create a better planet for all of us; spirituality can lift us out of any kind of confusion, misery, or failure. He was truly a patriot because he said that one should use one’s degree to transform the society where one lives. In 2012, he started a movement, What Can I Give, to combat corruption, environmental degradation, and social evils.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. Faith and fact can, together, create a better planet.
answer: It means that this fact helps us understand some basic knowledge of the planet and the providence from which we can see how our creations and discoveries continue. So if these two are used perfectly, they could definitely create a better planet for all of us.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. Dr. Kalam’s own life was nourished by multiple faiths.
answer: Dr. Kalam’s father’s best friends were of different faiths. P. L. Shastrigal was the Head priest of the Rameswara temple, whereas Father Boda was the Head priest of the first church on Rameswara island. Dr. Kalam’s father himself was a boatman who also served as an imam at the local mosque. Very often, the three would sit together, despite their religious differences, to sort out problems affecting their community. Their unity and friendship had a deep impact on Dr. Kalam. Life was nourished by multiple faiths.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. But there was a major roadblock.
answer: Dr. Vikram Sarabhai, the great scientist from ISRO, selected a site in Thumba, Kerala, to set up a Space Research Centre because of its proximity to the magnetic equator. However, there was a problem obtaining that piece of land since it housed a fishing ground for the locals, an old church, the Bishop’s house and a school.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression conveys in the context they occur in the text. Only when the dust is removed, does the mirror shine and the reflection becomes clear
answer: It means that in our soul, there are lots of impurities of greed, selfishness, lust and it makes us bad or evil people. When we can remove this dust by living pure and ethically and by serving humanity, then God will shine through us.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. Through his (Kalam’s) words of wisdom, I was getting to learn from countless great minds.
answer: Dr. Kalam was a deep thinker. Once he was discussing with the author about Prof. Dhawan’s advice on leading a pure life. Prof. Dhawan had said that one can remove impurities from one’s soul by living a pure and ethical life and serving humanity. At this juncture, the author remembered how this was very similar to what Dr. Kalam had told him on the day of his graduation, that he should use his knowledge to serve society. That is when he realised that through Dr. Kalam, he was indirectly gaining wisdom from the countless great people who had interacted with Dr. Kalam.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. He gathered the sands of conscience to be our guide, our best friend.
answer: Dr. Kalam was receptive to the learning of great and noble minds. All the same, he would refine the learning using his conscience. He believed that our conscience is our guide and best friend when it comes to our behaviour.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Write in your own simple words what the following expression convey in the context they occur in the text. ‘What can I take?’ is the thought which is responsible for all the wrong, seen around us.
answer: Man tends to use resources for his progress and glory. His greedy nature, constantly taking from nature, damages it and harms other living things. Thus, the tendency to take only leads to the depletion of resources and causes harm to the environment as well as society.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

government officials: The government officials were instructed to ensure that the State elections are conducted in a free and fair manner.
betterment of humanity: Every country must try and prevent environmental degradation for the betterment of humanity.
pure and ethical life: It is necessary to listen to one’s conscience to lead a pure and ethical life.
community’s problems: Global problems would largely reduce, if each and every community’s problems are resolved promptly.
religious service: The truest form of religious service is to selflessly help those in need.
astonishing aspect: The most astonishing aspect of Baba Amte’s personality was his humble nature.
technological adyancement: India has made rapid technological advancement in the field of Defence and National security.
frank dialogue: Conflicts and misunderstandings at any level can be sorted through peaceful and frank dialogues.
social evils: Though India is one of the most influential nations, we still have various social evils that hinder our growth.
faith curbs: Faith curbs the greed and animosity in man to a great extent.
inquisitive disciple: Karan was Prof. Ansari’s most inquisitive disciple as he wanted to learn everything about psychology from him.
academic brilliance: Academic brilliance is a quality many parents desire in their children.
environmental degradation: Man has made rapid progress in many spheres but all at the cost of environmental degradation.
pin-drop silence: As soon as the Principal entered the classroom, there was pin-drop silence.
alternate accommodation: As our present building was to be reconstructed, all the families had to look out for an alternate accommodation.
remarkable person: Satyajit Ray was not only a cinematic genius, but also a remarkable person.
silent motivation: The promise of a better tomorrow became a silent motivation for a lot of freedom fighters in the pre-independence era.
famished bodies: Excess food during social gatherings must be donated appropriately to feed the famished bodies in our society.
unsuccessful launch: Had Dr. Kalam not considered the unsuccessful launch as a stepping stone, he would have probably never experienced success in the future.
space research centre: All prospective space researchers struggle to be a part of NASA, the most celebrated space research centre.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Copy the first paragraph on page 92 in your notebook, carefully. Encircle all the puctuation marks with a coloured pencil/pen. “Dear children ...................... ‘amen’.
answer: ‘Dear children, Dr. Vikram says that, within a year, he wants to build scientific facilities near the sea coast, replacing all the settlements that now stand there. Now, can you give up your abode? Can I give up my abode? Can we give up God’s abode for such a great scientific mission?’ Dr. Kalam recalled how he witnessed ‘a pin drop silence for a long moment’. Then everyone got up, and the whole church reverberated with the deafening noise of a collective ‘amen’.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Read Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam’s very renowned books ‘Wings of Fire’; and ‘Ignited Minds’, to find the following: (a) Subject of the book (b) Special features of the book
answer:
Ignited Minds by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam.

Subject of the book: Motivating Indians especially the Indian youth to dream big and to believe that what they dream can be true.
Special features of the book: It is a short book of 205 pages with 9 chapters. He writes about why we are not developing as we should have and how we will become a developed country. He inspires the Indians to changes the thinking and attitude of the country forging India to become a developed country.
Teachings/learnings/moral/message from the book: The book consists of many inspirational messages from great people. This book is meant for Indian children to ignite their thinking and help them think big for our country. Well-inspired stories are provided in each chapter to encourage and build a spirit of oneness. It contains dynamic and original ideas, examines attitudes afflicting the Indians, and presents a prescription for the rapid growth of India to enable the country to emerge as a developed country.
Our opinion/idea/comment on the book: The book is written in simple, easy-to-understand language and inspires everyone who reads it. The books can ignite the fire in the minds of anyone who reads and understands them.

Wings of Fire by APJ Abdul Kalam

Subject of the book: Wings of Fire is the autobiography of APJ Abdul Kalam, former President of India. It was published in 1999, and it covers Kalam’s life before he became the President of India.
Special features: The book covers his early life and his work in Indian space research and missile programs. It is the story of a boy from a humble background who went on to become a great scientist and later the president of India. The Book contains very motivational quotes. It also provides a glimpse of the harmony that existed in India before its partition. The book also contains many of his poems.
Teachings/learnings/moral/message: One can find a glimpse of Kalam’s positive thinking throughout the book. He held many high-ranking positions in various organisations, yet he has rarely mentioned anything about corruption or ignorance of bureaucracy or politicians. The secret to his success seems to be his ability to ignore negative things around him. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam is simple, secular, and inspiring.
Our opinion/comment: Wings of Fire is an autobiography of Dr. Kalam in simple language. His words helped many youngsters realise that a fire exists in each and every one of us, and that it has to be utilised in the right way. How should the young blood react? What are our duties as citizens? has been depicted very beautifully in simple but elegant words that have attracted many readers, including me.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Draft a speech that you would give at your School Assembly convincing junior students that the secret of true happiness lies in Giving and Sharing more than in Receiving and Taking.
answer:
The Spirit of Giving
Good morning, respected Principal, teachers and my dear friends. I, Ms. Srnriti Patel, am here to talk to you about ‘the spirit of giving’. All religions across the globe emphasise the importance of charity for self-cleansing and leading a quality life. God gives, parents give, nature gives, and they do it selflessly. History quotes one of the great Presidents of the USA as saying, “Ask not what your country can do for you, and rather ask what you can do for your country.”
However, our selfishness prevents us from sharing and giving. We accumulate and hoard for a tomorrow which may never come. We spoil the present for others, depriving them of a better today. In doing so, the quality of life is lost, and all we’re left with is a rat race that leads nowhere.
So, let’s mend our ways. Let’s give. For in giving, there is care, happiness and satisfaction. Let’s take an oath today to heal this selfish world by changing our way of thinking from ‘Receiving and Taking’ to ‘Giving and Sharing’. Thank you.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Divide your class in two groups and have a Debate on the topic ‘Science and Spirituality can go hand in hand’.
answer:
For (view):
Both science and spirituality study aspects of our world. Both require mental discipline. Both require belief in certain axioms and truths. Hence both are valid. Science is the body. Spirituality is the mind. Science explores the creations of God. Spirituality delves into the nature of God. Both are two sides of the same coin. Both complement each other. It is inevitable therefore that science and spirituality go hand in hand.
Against (counterview): 
Science requires factual knowledge. It is a component of millions of physical laws. Spirituality is a free bird that soars on the wings of personal belief. It does not require proof. Only faith. How then can they be considered to be on the same plane? One is concrete; the other abstract. In order to draw upon spirituality, one may conjure up an image in the mind and be satisfied. Science seeks explanation only in logical reasoning based upon unlimited past experiences and developed into truth by considering an experiment after experiment two are completely different in nature and cannot go hand in hand.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.6 - Science and Spirituality

question: Get into pairs and discuss the following with your partners and complete the table. Many people are superstitious. This means that they have belief for which they have no logical reason.
answer:
Superstition
What it implies

(1) Smashing a mirror
Brings seven years of bad luck.

(2) Knocking on wood
Prevents disappointment.

(3) Hanging a horseshoe
Brings good luck.

(4) Finding a four-leaf clover leaf
Brings good luck.

(5) Bird droppings on the head
Signals monetary gain.

(6) If a cat crosses one’s path
Your tasks get delayed.

(7) Hanging a lemon with seven green chillies
Wards off misfortune.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.1 - Night of the Scorpion

question: Brainstorm what you know about Scorpions. Use the points given below. Scorpions How they attack Where they live Their appearance Any more information about them
answer:
Where they live: Scorpions are generally found in deserts, semi-arid regions, under logs, rocks, boards and clutter. The Bark Scorpion lives on vertical surfaces such as tree bark, walls, or bushes.
Their appearance: Scorpions have eight legs, two pedipalps, and a tail with a venom-injecting barb. Scorpions have two venom glands that produce venom used for hunting and self-defence. Scorpions do not have bones; instead, they have an exoskeleton made of chitin, which is similar to the shell of a shrimp.
How they attack: Scorpions stay motionless beneath stones, wood, bark, and other objects on the ground that allow them to conceal themselves until the prey is close enough to be caught quickly. They can also oriefly chase down a detected victim and attack at the right moment. Scorpions do not go out looking for · food; their success depends on waiting. They do not have excellent vision, but through their sensory hair, they can know what kind of animal is approaching and how far it is from them.
More information about scorpions: Scorpions have unbelievable survival skills. Researchers have frozen scorpions overnight and then left them out in the sun, only to see them thaw and crawl away. They can live in the worst environments. A scorpion’s survival is challenged in the absence of soil. Since they are burrowing animals, the life of a scorpion is endangered in case there is frost or dense grass on the ground, and the scorpion’s access to soil is cut off.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.1 - Night of the Scorpion

question: After reading the poem, complete the following. What happens? There are three main parts of the poem. Do you know what they are about? The first one is done for you.
answer:
Lines
What is happening?

1-7
The scorpion comes into the home to escape the rain and stings the poet’s mother.

8-33
The peasants try all the superstitions associated with a scorpion’s bite, so that they can heal the poet’s mother from the effects of its venom.

34-48
The poet's father tries to tell the poet’s mother about her pain. When the scorpion’s sting is finally lost, the poet’s mother is thankful that it did not sting her children.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.1 - Night of the Scorpion

Imagery

Look at the description of the village peasants.
What does the imagery suggest about them?

The Images
What images suggest

They came like swarms of flies.
They came in huge numbers.

They buzzed the name of God.
They chanted the name of God to relieve the poet’s mother from the pain of the scorpion’s sting.

They threw giant scorpion shadows on the mud-baked walls.
They searched the entire house for the scorpion with lanterns and candles, casting giant scorpion shadows on the walls and scaring the villagers.

They clicked their tongues.
They collectively made sounds to prevent the scorpion from coming close to them.

the attempts by the peasants to help alleviate the mother’s pain.

“May he sit still, they said,
May the sins of your previous birth
be burned away tonight, they said.

the misfortunes of your next birth, they said.

balanced in the unreal world
against the sum of good.
become diminished by your pain.

of desire, and your spirit of ambition”
